imageAlt: French infantry in a forward position at Verdun, 1916
imageCaption: French infantry at Verdun, 1916. The battle ran for 303 days and cycled 259 of France's 330 divisions through the Voie Sacrée supply corridor.

longForm.title: The Furnace at Verdun: How France and Germany Built an Attrition Machine Neither Could Turn Off
longForm.excerpt: In February 1916, Germany attacked Verdun not to take it but to force France to bleed defending it. The plan worked on both sides simultaneously.
paragraphs: The Meuse runs north through eastern France and bends near Verdun, creating a salient the city has occupied since the Romans called it Virodunum. The right bank of the river rises into a series of ridges and fortified heights: Fort Douaumont at 388 meters, Fort Vaux below and east of it, the Pepper Hill complex behind. On the left bank, across the river, the Cote 304 and its southern neighbor called Mort Homme — Dead Man's Hill — commanded the valley with direct observation over the right bank. Any attacker who held the right bank but not the left was exposing his flank to artillery with a clear line of sight. This geography mattered in ways the German planners understood and the French commanders were too slow to act on.

General Erich von Falkenhayn's memorandum to the Kaiser in December 1915 proposed an operation at Verdun that was not, in conventional terms, designed to capture ground. The French, Falkenhayn argued, could not allow Verdun to fall. Its loss would be strategically irrelevant but symbolically catastrophic. Therefore, if Germany threatened it with sufficient force, France would commit its reserves to defend it. Germany's purpose was not to take Verdun; it was to kill French soldiers at a rate France could not sustain. The phrase attributed to Falkenhayn, 'bleed France white,' may be apocryphal. The concept was not. What Falkenhayn had designed was an attrition trap. He had not modeled what would happen when his own forces bled at the same rate.

At 0715 on February 21, 1916, the German Fifth Army opened fire along an eight-mile front with 1,220 artillery pieces. For the next nine hours, the French forward positions ceased to exist as organized defensive lines. Approximately two million shells fell in the first day's bombardment. What remained when German infantry crossed the start line that afternoon were fragments: isolated men in shell craters, communication trenches that no longer communicated with anyone, and forward observers with no way to report what they were seeing. The French had six weeks' warning that an offensive was coming. They had done comparatively little with it.

Four days into the battle, on February 25, a patrol of Brandenburg Pioneers — approximately a dozen men under Sergeant Kunze, later joined by two officers — entered Fort Douaumont through an unguarded caponier and found it held by a handful of elderly territorials. The fort had been stripped of its garrison to feed the front. Kunze and his men locked the French soldiers in the magazine and took the fort. The strongest concrete fortification in France fell to a patrol that had gotten lost. German newspapers reported a triumph of arms. The strategic effect, securing the commanding heights of the right bank, was real. The tactical reality was an accident that would take France nine months and enormous expenditure of blood to reverse.

General Philippe Petain assumed command at Verdun on February 26, one day after Douaumont fell. His first act was logistical. The single road connecting Verdun to Bar-le-Duc, what would be called the Voie Sacree, was the only supply route that had not been cut by German artillery. Petain organized a convoy system along its 56 kilometers: a truck every 14 seconds at peak operation, 6,000 vehicles moving 50,000 tons of supplies and 90,000 men per week. Road maintenance crews worked continuously. Then Petain instituted the noria system, rotating divisions through Verdun on a systematic schedule rather than leaving them until they were destroyed. The intent was to spread the attrition across the French Army rather than concentrate it on units already engaged. Of France's 330 divisions, 259 would cycle through Verdun. The system worked. It also meant that most of the French Army spent part of 1916 in the furnace.

By March, the German attack had a tactical problem it had not anticipated. French artillery on the left bank, on Cote 304 and Mort Homme, was firing into the flanks of German forces on the right bank from positions they could not suppress without attacking across the river. Falkenhayn had not planned to attack the left bank. He now had no choice. The fighting for Mort Homme began in March and ran through May. The hill changed hands repeatedly. German forces would take the forward crest and find French artillery on the reverse slope still firing. The attack had expanded beyond its original design, and neither side could disengage. This is what happens when an operation built around limited objectives encounters terrain that refuses to cooperate with the plan.

Fort Vaux fell on June 7, 1916, after a seven-day siege conducted largely underground. Major Sylvain-Eugene Raynal commanded roughly 600 men inside the fort. German assault teams worked through the tunnel system with flamethrowers, grenades, and gas in spaces too narrow to stand upright. Raynal's men fought room by room. His water supply was exhausted by June 4. He sent carrier pigeons requesting relief; the last one arrived at divisional headquarters poisoned by gas but still flying, and died after delivering its message. On June 7, Raynal surrendered. German Crown Prince Wilhelm, who received him, treated him with the ceremony due a defeated officer who had defended his position until nothing remained to defend. The fort had cost the attacking force roughly ten times what the garrison defending it had cost France.

On June 23, 1916, German forces reached Fleury-devant-Douaumont, approximately four kilometers from the city of Verdun itself. This was as close as they would get. The advance had consumed 22 divisions and produced gains measured in hundreds of meters per week. The Somme offensive began on July 1, forcing German reserves westward. More practically, the German Army at Verdun had fought itself to the same exhaustion it had been designed to inflict on the French. The machine had run in both directions simultaneously. Falkenhayn was relieved of command in August and replaced by Hindenburg and Ludendorff. The operation designed to be strategically controlled had become a commitment neither side could terminate.

General Robert Nivelle and General Charles Mangin planned the French counteroffensives of October and December 1916. What had changed was artillery doctrine. The French developed and refined the creeping barrage — rolling artillery fire that advanced ahead of the infantry at a pace the infantry could follow. Fort Douaumont was retaken on October 24, nine months after it was lost. Fort Vaux on November 2. The final French offensive in December pushed German lines back to roughly where they had been in February. The operational logic was sound: precise artillery preparation, clearly defined limited objectives, infantry following the barrage rather than waiting for it to lift. The same Nivelle, applying the same methods to the Chemin des Dames offensive in 1917, would produce mutiny. At Verdun they worked because the objectives were achievable and the ground defensible.

The Battle of Verdun ran for 303 days. French casualties: approximately 377,000, of whom roughly 162,000 were killed. German casualties: approximately 337,000, of whom roughly 100,000 were killed. The front lines at the end of 1916 were nearly identical to the front lines at the beginning. Falkenhayn's design, to bleed France white, had succeeded in attritional terms and failed in strategic ones: Germany bled at approximately the same rate it inflicted. No country wins a war of attrition against an adversary with equivalent reserve capacity, equivalent industrial support, and a supply network capable of rotating divisions faster than they can be destroyed. This was not a difficult proposition to model in advance. It was a difficult conclusion to accept in an army whose doctrine centered on offensive decision. Verdun did not change that doctrine. It simply demonstrated, at a cost of roughly 700,000 casualties, that the doctrine had nothing to offer the problem.

shortForm.title: Operation Cobra: The Breakout That Required a Disaster to Succeed
shortForm.excerpt: On July 25, 1944, American bombers killed 111 of their own men near St-Lo. The ground commander attacked anyway, and broke out of Normandy in four days.
paragraphs: The Norman bocage — the dense hedgerow country inland from the beaches — was terrain that American armored doctrine had not accounted for. Each field in the Calvados countryside was bounded by earthen banks topped with dense root systems, some centuries old and several feet thick. Tanks could not push through; climbing over exposed the belly to anti-tank fire. Infantry had to clear each field separately. Six weeks after D-Day, American forces were fighting for ground measured in hundreds of yards. General Omar Bradley needed a way through the hedgerows. The solution was to concentrate enough air power on a narrow sector to open a gap before the Germans could seal it. The plan was called Operation Cobra.

The attack was scheduled for July 24, postponed for weather, and partially executed anyway when the recall did not reach all aircraft in time. On July 25, 1,500 heavy bombers of the Eighth Air Force attacked along the St-Lo to Periers road, the northern boundary of the target box. Bombs fell short. 111 American soldiers were killed and 490 wounded by friendly fire, among them Lieutenant General Lesley McNair — the highest-ranking American officer killed in the European theater. General J. Lawton Collins, commanding VII Corps, faced a choice: halt the attack and lose the window, or push through the damage and exploit whatever gap the bombing had created. Collins attacked.

The Panzer Lehr Division, which had held the sector, had been effectively destroyed. Its commander, General Fritz Bayerlein, later described the post-bombing position as a moonscape. American armor moved through the gap on July 26. Within four days, VII Corps had reached Avranches at the base of the Cotentin Peninsula. General Patton's Third Army was activated August 1 with orders to exploit south and east. The breakout from the bocage that had seemed unreachable for six weeks was complete in less than a week. The bombing had worked, badly and at a cost, and Collins had been right to press the attack despite it.

What Cobra demonstrated was the fragility of a defensive line once coherent fire control is lost. Panzer Lehr was not poorly commanded or poorly equipped; it was hit by a weight of ordnance no defensive position could absorb and then attacked by armor before it could reconstitute. The tactical lesson — that carpet bombing creates an exploitation window that closes within hours — shaped American combined-arms doctrine for the rest of the war. The harder lesson, which took longer to absorb, was narrower: using heavy strategic bombers in close support of ground troops without precision and without adequate standoff distance will kill your own men. The solution to the bocage problem worked. The method that delivered it was not repeatable without accepting the same cost.

onThisDay.headline: Benito Mussolini executed by Communist partisans near Lake Como
onThisDay.summary: Italian dictator Benito Mussolini was captured at Dongo while attempting to flee to Switzerland concealed in a German military convoy, and shot that afternoon at the Villa Belmonte in Giulino di Mezzegra alongside his mistress Clara Petacci. The execution was carried out by Communist partisan Walter Audisio. Their bodies were transported to Milan and displayed upside down at Piazzale Loreto — the same location where Fascist authorities had publicly displayed the bodies of resistance fighters the previous August. Mussolini had ruled Italy as dictator since 1922 and led the country into the Second World War as a German ally in June 1940.
